data.title: Live updates: Dueling Senate health care plans fail; Indiana redistricting push tests Republicans
Carville weighs in on Trump: ‘He’s done. It’s over’
data.description:
Democratic strategist James Carville on Wednesday said President Trump’s controversial comments about immigrants have begun to lose traction with voters ahead of the 2026 midterm elections.

“He’s done. It’s over. You’re a loser, dude. You’re losing everywhere, and you’re going to lose more because you, my friend, are a loser,” Carville said of Trump’s ability to resonate with voters during a Wednesday episode of “Politicon.” 

Carville ventured to put his hand in the shape of an “L” — a sign for “loser” — for the president. The longtime Democratic adviser said Trump’s remarks about Rep. Ilhan Omar (D-Minn.) and the Somali community along with his administration’s harsh immigration policies will impact campaigns next year.

“He’s paying for it, and he’s going to continue to pay for it,” Carville said during the show. 

“And we got to stop looking at him as some kind of political Svengali or political Houdini, or anything like that. He’s done. We just got to butter this toast and slice it and eat it. He’s done,” he continued.

His comments come days after Trump lauded his administration’s treatment of Somali immigrants.

“I love this Ilhan Omar, whatever the hell her name is. With her little turban. I love her. She comes in, does nothing but b—-. She’s always complaining. She comes from her country where, I mean it’s considered about the worst country in the world,” Trump said, during a rally that was billed as focused on lowering costs for Americans, as his supporters in the room jeered.

Trump later touted that he had paused migration from “hellholes like Afghanistan, Haiti, Somalia, and many other countries.”

Media outlets, immigration advocacy groups and others have slammed Trump for his rhetoric.

“The fact that Trump would demonize an entire diaspora — the vast majority of whom live here as legal citizens or permanent residents — is beyond reprehensible. It’s dangerous,” The Minnesota Star Tribune wrote in an editorial condemning Trump’s remarks.

Carville said voters were taking notice.

“People are seeing this, and people are voting on this,” the Democratic strategist said.

data.title: Greene on Epstein files release: ‘I’ll only believe it when I see it’
data.description:
Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) said that when it comes to the full release of files linked with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, she’ll “only believe it when” she “sees it.”

“Are you confident that the Trump administration will release the full Epstein files?” CBS News’s Nikole Killion asked Greene in a clip posted to the social platform X on Thursday.

“I’ll only believe it when I see it, and that’s how I feel about government in every capacity. And I think that’s where Americans are too,” Greene responded.

“They no longer trust what they are told in some meme or some red meat rant speech or some ad they see from a, from a candidate or a politician. I’ll believe it when I see it, and I really hope the Trump administration does the right thing,” she added.

President Trump last month signed the Epstein Files Transparency Act, ordering the Department of Justice (DOJ) to release every unclassified record and document linked to Epstein.

Trump’s action was a notable reversal, with him previously being strongly against releasing the files.

Greene has said Trump was enraged with her in the wake of her signing a House petition pushing for the release of all government files linked to Epstein. 

“We did talk about the Epstein files and he was extremely angry at me that I signed the discharge petition to release the files,” Greene told CBS News in a “60 Minutes” interview.

“I fully believe those women deserve everything they’re asking. They’re asking for it to come out. They deserve it.”

Greene’s relationship with the president has gone drastically downhill in the last few weeks, with Trump announcing in mid-November he was revoking his endorsement and support of Greene and would back a primary challenger to her.

“President Trump has been consistently calling for transparency related to the Epstein files and his Administration has delivered — by releasing thousands of pages of documents, cooperating with the House Oversight Committee’s subpoena request, and President Trump recently calling for further investigations into Epstein’s Democrat friends, the Trump Administration has done more for the victims than Democrats ever have,” White House spokesperson Abigail Jackson said in a Thursday email to The Hill.

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.

data.title: DOJ fails a second time in securing new Letitia James indictment from grand jury
data.description:
The Justice Department has failed again to secure a new indictment against New York Attorney General Letitia James (D) after its initial case against her was dismissed, The Associated Press reported, the second time a grand jury has refused to approve charges this month. 

James’s case was dismissed after a judge found the appointment of Lindsey Halligan as acting U.S. attorney was unlawful, and despite first vowing to appeal, prosecutors have since sought to proceed with a new case.   

A panel of grand jurors last week rejected an indictment on the charges pursued by prosecutors, to which James responded by reiterating her stance that the prosecution against her is “baseless.” 

The New York attorney general has cast herself as a target of selective and vindictive prosecution at President Trump’s direction. She became one of his foremost political foes after taking on his business empire in Manhattan. 

The Justice Department had no comment on the development. The U.S. Attorney’s office for Eastern District of Virginia did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The Hill also requested comment from James’s legal team and office.  

DEVELOPING

data.title: Cruz: Crockett ‘the face of the Democratic Party’
data.description:
Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas) said Rep. Jasmine Crockett (D-Texas) was the “face” of the Democratic Party after she launched a bid for the Senate earlier this week. 

“I don’t know Jasmine Crockett, I’ve never met her, but I think she is the face of the Democratic Party. And I welcome her to this race,” Cruz said during a Wednesday evening appearance on Fox News’s “Hannity.”

“You know, it says something about where today’s Democrats are, that the fellow I did run against, Colin Allred, had to drop out of the Democrat primary the instant Jasmine Crockett jumped in, because in their party, they want crazy, and they want more and more crazy,” he added.

Republicans have railed against Crockett, who’s built a name off of bucking the Trump administration’s policies and the president’s GOP supporters, during frequent appearances on cable news shows.

Crockett used audio from President Trump in her campaign debut video, which showcased him describing her as “low-IQ.”

The president doubled down on those remarks following her announcement.

“She’s a low-IQ person. I can’t imagine she wins,” Trump told reporters while en route to a speech in Pennsylvania. 

“Maybe she’ll get the Democratic nomination, but I think it’s a gift to Republicans. … I’ve watched her for the last two years. I can’t even believe she’s a politician, actually,” he continued.

On Wednesday, Cruz shared similar sentiments, predicting that if Democrats continue to put Crockett in front of voters, she’d botch their chances of winning midterm elections.

“I actually think Jasmine Crockett and [former Vice President] Kamala Harris and [Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez] AOC and [New York Mayor-elect Zohran] Mamdani ought to go on a national tour,” Cruz said.

“I’m going to tell Jasmine, don’t just campaign in Texas. We need you in every swing state across the country in the midterms. We need you to deliver and keep a Republican House and Republican Senate. So please travel the country.”

data.title: Trump allies ramp up pressure on Indiana GOP ahead of final redistricting vote
data.description:
High-profile Republicans ramped up pressure on Indiana Republican senators to pass a new GOP-favored map on Thursday as the lawmakers convened in the state legislature to weigh whether to pass new congressional lines ahead of 2026. 

“Rod Bray, the Senate leader in Indiana, has consistently told us he wouldn’t fight redistricting while simultaneously whipping his members against it,” Vice President Vance wrote on X., referring to Indiana Senate President Pro Tem Rodric Bray. “That level of dishonesty cannot be rewarded, and the Indiana GOP needs to choose a side.” 

Vance was responding to a post on X from Donald Trump, Jr., who warned that he would be primarying any GOP holdouts who voted against redrawing the Hoosier State’s map. 

“If Indiana Republicans side with these Never Trumpers to do the dirty work of Democrats, I’ll be spending a lot of time in Indiana next year campaigning against every single one of them. PS: These RINO consultants sabotaging MAGA need to be rooted out of the GOP!!!” Trump Jr. wrote ahead of the session.  

The conservative Heritage Action suggested that the president had threatened to withhold federal funding from Indiana if it failed to pass a new map in a post on X ahead of their session.

“Roads will not be paved. Guard bases will close. Major projects will stop. These are the stakes and every NO vote will be to blame,” the lobbying arm of the Heritage Foundation wrote. 

2024 Election Coverage

  • 2024 Election News
  • 2024 Election Polling Data
  • 2024 Presidential Election Forecast

Indiana senators are convening in the state legislature to weigh whether to pass a 9-0 GOP-favored House map, which would give Republicans two pickup opportunities ahead of 2026. 

The White House and GOP allies have waged a public pressure campaign to push state lawmakers to pass new lines as Republicans look to net pickup opportunities in the House as they brace for a challenging midterm environment next year.  

The vote in the Indiana Senate is being heavily watched as the caucus has signaled multiple times that passage in the upper chamber is not a given. Several Republican senators on the floor suggested they might vote against a new map.

data.title: Newsom: Trump most destructive president in my life
data.description:
California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) on Wednesday accused President Trump of being the nation’s “most destructive” president ever during an appearance on New York Times columnist Ezra Klein’s podcast.

“I think Trump is one of the most destructive presidents and human beings in my lifetime,” Newsom said. “I think this republic is at real risk, this country being unrecognizable. And I have no patience for people that want to indulge it.”

Newsom expressed frustration at financial, educational and political entities that “are sitting there bending the knee to this president.”

“I can’t stand the crony capitalism,” the California Democrat continued. “…I can’t stand the universities have done that, the law firms that have done that, individual corporate leaders that have done that, other governors, maybe Democrats and Republicans, that have been complicit at this moment.”

“This guy is reckless,” Newsom told Klein. “We’ll not have a fair and free election if we don’t continue to fight. I’m the future ex-governor. Who the hell knows what happens the rest of my life? Except one thing I know that matters in the rest of my life is I have to look at my kids in the goddamn eye. I mean that seriously.”

Newsom, a possible 2028 Democratic presidential contender, has been a vocal critic of Trump’s since his first term. The governor escalated his critiques following Trump’s return to the White House, using his platform on social media to mock and mimic Trump’s online presence.

The governor said he had “softened” on the threat of Trump running for a third term despite constitutional limits. He said in an interview on “The Jack Cocchiarella Show” on Sunday that Trump’s “regime is going to be measured not in decades, but in years.” After an audible sigh, Newsom added, “And that’s comforting.”

The governor has also mocked Trump with AI-generated videos, including a recent one depicting him, Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth and White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller crying while in handcuffs.

During the government shutdown and the demolition of the White House’s East Wing to make way for a ballroom, Newsom shared an AI-generated image of Marie Antoinette with Trump’s face.

“TRUMP ‘MARIE ANTOINETTE’ SAYS, ‘NO HEALTH CARE FOR YOU PEASANTS, BUT A BALLROOM FOR THE QUEEN!’” the post on the social platform X read.

Trump has regularly fired back in the other direction.

The president called on Newsom to resign and blamed him for the massive wildfires that tore through Los Angeles in January. In June, Trump supported arresting Newsom after the governor dared White House border czar Tom Homan to arrest him following federal immigration raids in Los Angeles. 

The president has also shared memes and videos mocking Newsom. One in September shows the California Democrat giving a press conference with a red “Trump 2028” hat superimposed over his head.

data.title: US imposes new sanctions on Maduro family members, oil ships
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday announced new sanctions on the family members of Venezuelan strong-man Nicolas Maduro and Venezuelan businesses, a day after the U.S. seized a Venezuelan oil tanker in an escalation of the administration’s pressure campaign on the country.

The U.S. is imposing sanctions on three nephews of Maduro’s wife, Cilia Flores; a Maduro-affiliated businessman; and six shipping companies operating in Venezuela’s oil sector. 

The administration is framing the sanctions as striking at Maduro’s enablers in Venezuela.

“Nicolas Maduro and his criminal associates in Venezuela are flooding the United States with drugs that are poisoning the American people,” Secretary of the Treasury Scott Bessent said in a statement announcing the sanctions. 

“Under President Trump’s leadership, Treasury is holding the regime and its circle of cronies and companies accountable for its continued crimes.”

The new sanctions come on top of an escalating military campaign against alleged drug-boats that the administration said are coming from Venezuela at the direction of Maduro, who is indicted in the U.S. for drug-trafficking and other offenses. 

All property and interests in the U.S. held by the sanctioned individuals is blocked and must be reported to the Treasury’s Office of Foreign Assets Control. 

The administration earlier justified its seizure of a Venezuelan oil tanker as enforcing sanctions blocking Venezuela’s oil exports. 

“I think the president considers the seizure of the oil tanker as affectuating the administration’s sanction policies,” White House Spokesperson Karoline Leavitt said on Thursday. 

Trump has earlier said that the U.S. would move to “land” action in Venezuela, raising the possibility of an American military invasion.

data.title: Florida attorney general sues Starbucks over ‘reverse discrimination’
data.description:
TALLAHASSEE, Fla. (WFLA) — Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier (R) filed a lawsuit against Starbucks, claiming the company’s hiring initiatives constitute racial discrimination.

“Starbucks made DEI more than a slogan,” the attorney general said in a video announcing the lawsuit Wednesday. “They turned it into a mandatory hiring and promotion system based on race.”

The complaint states that in 2024, multiple Florida residents who worked for or tried to apply for jobs at Starbucks contacted Uthmeier’s office to report feeling “excluded” due to the company’s employment practices.

One person who claimed to have worked at the coffee giant for 17 years told the attorney general that the company paid higher wages to people of certain races more than others who had the same experience and skills, according to the lawsuit.

Uthmeier argued that these practices began in 2020 when the company announced an initiative to hire people of color in 40 percent of retail positions and 30 percent of corporate jobs by 2025. He claimed the company also created “race-based quotas” for suppliers and its board of directors and excluded people of certain races from networking and mentorship opportunities.

The last claim is in reference to a Starbucks mentorship program that the company said was aimed at connecting employees who are Black, Indigenous or people of color with senior leaders, according to previous reports by NBC.

“The coffee empire set numerical racial targets for their workforce, and they tied executive bonuses to those targets,” Florida’s top prosecutor said. “That is brazen discrimination, and it is against the law.”

The lawsuit refers to the company’s employment practices as “reverse discrimination,” and asks the court to bar Starbucks from carrying out these practices. Uthmeier is also asking for affirmative relief and damages, as well as civil penalties of $10,000 for each instance of racial discrimination against a Florida resident.

The attorney general said he estimates “tens of millions” of Sunshine State residents have been impacted, with over 900 Starbucks stores located throughout the state. He also specifically cites a store in Highlands County, though the connection is unclear.

“We disagree,” a spokesperson for Starbucks told Nexstar’s WFLA in a statement. “We are deeply committed to creating opportunity for every single one of our partners (employees).”

“Our programs and benefits are open to everyone and lawful,” they added. “Our hiring practices are inclusive, fair and competitive, and designed to ensure the strongest candidate for every job, every time.”

data.title: Older voters leaning toward Republicans ahead of midterms: Survey
data.description:
U.S. voters ages 50 and up are now trending Republican, less than a year ahead of the 2026 midterm elections, according to a new poll.

The Reuters/Ipsos survey, released Thursday, found that 46 percent of registered voters 50 and above say they support GOP candidates, while 38 percent prefer Democratic candidates. In 2022, the gap was 43-42, and in 2018, it was 40-38 in favor of Democrats.

Meanwhile, 42 percent of registered voters ages 18-49 support Democratic candidates, while 31 percent support Republicans. That margin has slimmed since 2022, when 48 percent of voters in the age group backed Democrats and 30 percent sided with the GOP. 

In the wake of recent victories in key elections this year — including the gubernatorial races in New Jersey and Virginia and the mayoral races in New York City and Miami, Democrats have expressed optimism about 2026 — particularly if their messaging about cost-of-living is able to break through again.

But older voters tend to turn out more frequently in midterm elections, with 64 percent of the electorate in 2022 comprised of voters ages 50 and older, according to the Pew Research Center. Seven in 10 GOP voters were older than 50, while 58 percent of Democratic voters were in that category. 

Republicans hold a 220-213 edge in the House and a 53-47 advantage in the Senate. Democrats need to flip three House seats and four Senate seats to gain control of each chamber.

2024 Election Coverage

  • 2024 Election News
  • 2024 Election Polling Data
  • 2024 Presidential Election Forecast

In the latest poll, 38 percent of voters ages 50 and up viewed cost of living as their primary concern. Another 27 percent said democratic values, 19 percent said immigration and 7 percent said crime.

Just over half of respondents ages 18-49, on the other hand, said cost of living is the most important factor in determining how they will vote. Just over 20 percent named democratic values, while 12 percent said immigration and 5 percent said crime.

The Reuters/Ipsos survey was conducted from Dec. 3-8 via online interviews with 3,521 registered voters. It has a margin of error of plus or minus 2 percentage points.
